lines.speaker: OCTAVIUS
lines.text: Now, Antony, our hopes are answered:

lines.speaker: OCTAVIUS
lines.text: You said the enemy would not come down,

lines.speaker: OCTAVIUS
lines.text: But keep the hills and upper regions:

lines.speaker: OCTAVIUS
lines.text: It proves not so: their battles are at hand;

lines.speaker: OCTAVIUS
lines.text: They mean to warn us at Philippi here,

lines.speaker: OCTAVIUS
lines.text: Answering before we do demand of them.

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Tut, I am in their bosoms, and I know

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Wherefore they do it: they could be content

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: To visit other places; and come down

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: With fearful bravery, thinking by this face

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: To fasten in our thoughts that they have courage;

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: But ’tis not so.

lines.speaker: MESSENGER
lines.text: Prepare you, generals:

lines.speaker: MESSENGER
lines.text: The enemy comes on in gallant show;

lines.speaker: MESSENGER
lines.text: Their bloody sign of battle is hung out,

lines.speaker: MESSENGER
lines.text: And something to be done immediately.

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Octavius, lead your battle softly on,

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Upon the left hand of the even field.

lines.speaker: OCTAVIUS
lines.text: Upon the right hand I; keep thou the left.

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Why do you cross me in this exigent?

lines.speaker: OCTAVIUS
lines.text: I do not cross you; but I will do so. March.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: They stand, and would have parley.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Stand fast, Titinius: we must out and talk.

lines.speaker: OCTAVIUS
lines.text: Mark Antony, shall we give sign of battle?

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: No, Caesar, we will answer on their charge.

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Make forth; the generals would have some words.

lines.speaker: OCTAVIUS
lines.text: Stir not until the signal.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Words before blows: is it so, countrymen?

lines.speaker: OCTAVIUS
lines.text: Not that we love words better, as you do.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Good words are better than bad strokes, Octavius.

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: In your bad strokes, Brutus, you give good words:

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Witness the hole you made in Caesar’s heart,

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Crying “Long live! hail, Caesar!”

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Antony,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: The posture of your blows are yet unknown;

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: But for your words, they rob the Hybla bees,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: And leave them honeyless.

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Not stingless too.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: O, yes, and soundless too;

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: For you have stol’n their buzzing, Antony,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: And very wisely threat before you sting.

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Villains, you did not so, when your vile daggers

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Hack’d one another in the sides of Caesar:

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: You show’d your teeth like apes, and fawn’d like hounds,

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: And bow’d like bondmen, kissing Caesar’s feet;

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Whilst damned Casca, like a cur, behind

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Struck Caesar on the neck. O you flatterers!

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Flatterers! Now, Brutus, thank yourself:

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: This tongue had not offended so to-day,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: If Cassius might have ruled.

lines.speaker: OCTAVIUS
lines.text: Come, come, the cause: if arguing make us sweat,

lines.speaker: OCTAVIUS
lines.text: The proof of it will turn to redder drops.

lines.speaker: OCTAVIUS
lines.text: Look;

lines.speaker: OCTAVIUS
lines.text: I draw a sword against conspirators;

lines.speaker: OCTAVIUS
lines.text: When think you that the sword goes up again?

lines.speaker: OCTAVIUS
lines.text: Never, till Caesar’s three and thirty wounds

lines.speaker: OCTAVIUS
lines.text: Be well avenged; or till another Caesar

lines.speaker: OCTAVIUS
lines.text: Have added slaughter to the sword of traitors.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Caesar, thou canst not die by traitors’ hands,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Unless thou bring’st them with thee.

lines.speaker: OCTAVIUS
lines.text: So I hope;

lines.speaker: OCTAVIUS
lines.text: I was not born to die on Brutus’ sword.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: O, if thou wert the noblest of thy strain,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Young man, thou couldst not die more honourable.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: A peevish schoolboy, worthless of such honour,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Join’d with a masker and a reveller!

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Old Cassius still!

lines.speaker: OCTAVIUS
lines.text: Come, Antony, away!

lines.speaker: OCTAVIUS
lines.text: Defiance, traitors, hurl we in your teeth:

lines.speaker: OCTAVIUS
lines.text: If you dare fight to-day, come to the field;

lines.speaker: OCTAVIUS
lines.text: If not, when you have stomachs.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Why, now, blow wind, swell billow and swim bark!

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: The storm is up, and all is on the hazard.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Ho, Lucilius! hark, a word with you.

lines.speaker: LUCILIUS
lines.text: Standing forth. My lord? Brutus and Lucilius converse apart.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Messala!

lines.speaker: MESSALA
lines.text: Standing forth. What says my general?

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Messala,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: This is my birth-day; as this very day

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Was Cassius born. Give me thy hand, Messala:

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Be thou my witness that against my will,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: As Pompey was, am I compell’d to set

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Upon one battle all our liberties.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: You know that I held Epicurus strong

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: And his opinion: now I change my mind,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: And partly credit things that do presage.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Coming from Sardis, on our former ensign

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Two mighty eagles fell, and there they perch’d,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Gorging and feeding from our soldiers’ hands;

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Who to Philippi here consorted us:

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: This morning are they fled away and gone;

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: And in their steads do ravens, crows and kites,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Fly o’er our heads and downward look on us,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: As we were sickly prey: their shadows seem

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: A canopy most fatal, under which

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Our army lies, ready to give up the ghost.

lines.speaker: MESSALA
lines.text: Believe not so.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: I but believe it partly;

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: For I am fresh of spirit and resolved

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: To meet all perils very constantly.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Even so, Lucilius.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Now, most noble Brutus,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: The gods to-day stand friendly, that we may,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Lovers in peace, lead on our days to age!

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: But since the affairs of men rest still incertain,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Let’s reason with the worst that may befall.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: If we do lose this battle, then is this

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: The very last time we shall speak together:

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: What are you then determined to do?

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Even by the rule of that philosophy

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: By which I did blame Cato for the death

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Which he did give himself, I know not how,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: But I do find it cowardly and vile,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: For fear of what might fall, so to prevent

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: The time of life: arming myself with patience

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: To stay the providence of some high powers

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: That govern us below.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Then, if we lose this battle,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: You are contented to be led in triumph

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Thorough the streets of Rome?

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: No, Cassius, no: think not, thou noble Roman,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: That ever Brutus will go bound to Rome;

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: He bears too great a mind. But this same day

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Must end that work the ides of March begun;

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: And whether we shall meet again I know not.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Therefore our everlasting farewell take:

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Forever, and forever, farewell, Cassius!

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: If we do meet again, why, we shall smile;

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: If not, why then, this parting was well made.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Forever, and forever, farewell, Brutus!

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: If we do meet again, we’ll smile indeed;

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: If not, ’tis true this parting was well made.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Why, then, lead on. O, that a man might know

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: The end of this day’s business ere it come!

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: But it sufficeth that the day will end,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: And then the end is known. Come, ho! away!

stage_directions.text: Enter Octavius, Antony, and their army.
Drum. Enter Brutus, Cassius, and their Army; Lucilius, Titinius, Messala, and others.
Exeunt Octavius, Antony, and their army.

glosses.definition: challenge us at Philippi (warn = summon to battle)
I am inside their confidence; I know their minds
a bravado that masks fear
the red flag (vexillum) raised before a Roman battle to announce engagement
a pressing moment; a critical juncture

glosses.phrase: I do not cross you; but I will do so
glosses.definition: Octavius's quiet assertion of independence from Antony — a precursor of the coming rupture between triumvirs
a formal conference between opposing commanders before battle, under flag of truce
respond when they attack
bees from Mount Hybla in Sicily — proverbial for producing the sweetest honey
bees buzz before stinging — Brutus's image for Antony's rhetoric

glosses.phrase: damned Casca, like a cur, behind
glosses.definition: damned Casca struck from behind like a dog — Antony's version of the assassination, emphasizing cowardice
Caesar's wound-count per Plutarch (who also gives 23 in another place); Shakespeare rounds up. The precise number mattered to Roman historians as a measure of ritualised violence
noblest of your family stock
petulant schoolboy — Cassius's contempt for Octavius, who was 19; the dismissal proves catastrophic, as Octavius becomes Augustus
a party-goer and carouser — Antony's public reputation for loose living
let the wave swell and the ship sail — nautical surrender to fortune
everything is at stake on the throw
Cassius's birthday — October 23 per Plutarch; he fights Philippi on his own birthday and dies on it
as Pompey was at Pharsalus (48 BCE) — compelled against his tactical judgment to stake everything on a single battle against Caesar. Cassius consciously invokes the precedent of the defeated side
Greek philosopher (341–270 BCE); his school rejected divine intervention and dismissed portents as superstition. Cassius is abandoning his Epicureanism in the face of the eagles' flight
the leading standard of the army
eagles perching on standards were an auspicious Roman omen; their departure is disastrous. Plutarch reports this incident
carrion birds — scavengers of battlefield dead; their presence is the Roman worst omen for an army
a most death-bringing canopy — the shadow of carrion birds overhead
die; yield up life
devoted friends in peacetime — Elizabethan 'lover' again; Cassius hopes they may live to old age as friends (not romantically)
Marcus Porcius Cato the Younger, Portia's father; committed suicide at Utica in 46 BCE rather than submit to Caesar. Brutus's in-law and model of Stoic self-killing
Brutus once considered suicide cowardly; he is about to revise his position (and does — he kills himself in 5.5)
steeling myself with patience — the Stoic virtue

glosses.phrase: stay the providence of some high powers
glosses.definition: await the decision of higher powers — Providence rather than self-determination
paraded as a defeated general in the victor's triumphal procession through Rome — a public humiliation that preceded execution

glosses.phrase: end that work the ides of March begun
glosses.definition: finish what the assassination began — Brutus expects to die, closing the circle
Brutus and Cassius's formal parting — the play's most affecting private moment between them

annotations.title: 'I do not cross you; but I will do so' — Octavius emerges
annotations.body:
The line is a single beat and a piece of political foreshadowing. Antony has just told Octavius to take the left wing; Octavius says he will take the right. Antony remonstrates; Octavius, precisely and mildly, refuses to yield: 'I do not cross you; but I will do so.' The sentence negates itself syntactically and asserts itself politically. For the first time in the play, Octavius stands as a figure who can overrule Antony.

This is the play's quietest piece of historical foreshadowing. Octavius — nineteen years old at Philippi — will by 31 BCE defeat Antony at Actium, by 27 BCE be Augustus, and rule as first emperor for 41 years. Cassius's contemptuous 'peevish schoolboy' (5.1 L60) is spoken of a teenager who will preside over the transition from Republic to Empire and survive into old age. Shakespeare gives the hinge one line. The reader who hears it hears the whole Augustan settlement turning on the words 'but I will do so.'
sources: Paul Zanker, *The Power of Images in the Age of Augustus* (1988)

annotations.title: Cassius on his birthday: the Epicurean who believes the eagles
annotations.body:
Cassius's birthday is 23 October; Philippi's first battle was fought on that date in 42 BCE, per Plutarch. Shakespeare preserves the coincidence and gives Cassius the Messala speech about the eagles, the ravens, and his abandoned Epicureanism. The speech is Cassius's most revealing moment of interior life.

Epicureanism, in the Roman sense Cassius had professed, is philosophically committed to a world without divine intervention and to treating omens as superstition. Cassius's admission that he has changed his mind on this — 'I held Epicurus strong / And his opinion: now I change my mind, / And partly credit things that do presage' — is a philosophical defection at the eve of the decisive battle of his life. The man who mocked Roman augurers in 1.2 and 1.3 now reads bird-flights as omens, and reads them correctly: his eagles have left, the carrion birds have come, and he will be dead by nightfall.

The play's philosophical map has been complex. Brutus is a Stoic who has argued against suicide (5.1 L101–108) and will commit suicide (5.5). Cassius is an Epicurean who has mocked omens (1.3) and now reads them faithfully. Each man ends the play having partly inverted his philosophy to meet his circumstances. Shakespeare is showing that the philosophical commitments that produced the conspiracy were theoretical; the circumstances that produced its end are not. Philosophy is unable to hold the shape events force on it.
sources: Miriam Griffin, 'Philosophy, Cato, and Roman Suicide', *G&R* 33 (1986)

annotations.title: 'Forever and forever farewell' — the play's most quietly affecting moment
annotations.body:
The exchange between Brutus and Cassius just before the battle — 'Forever, and forever, farewell, Cassius! / If we do meet again, why, we shall smile; / If not, why then, this parting was well made.' — is one of Shakespeare's best short elegies between two men who know they are unlikely to live out the day. The formality carries the feeling. Neither speaks of love directly; both use the register of republican-Roman farewell.

The scene turns also on the word 'lovers' again: 'The gods to-day stand friendly, that we may, / Lovers in peace, lead on our days to age!' Cassius uses the Elizabethan sense — devoted friends, trusted allies — in the same register Brutus will use in 3.2's oration. The word tracks through the play as a marker of the male political friendship that these two men have partly achieved and are about to lose.

Note also Brutus's revision on suicide. In response to Cassius's direct question — what will he do if they lose? — Brutus answers, initially, with a Stoic argument against suicide: he once blamed Cato for it and still finds it 'cowardly and vile.' But then, asked whether he will allow himself to be led in triumph through Rome, he answers 'No, Cassius, no... He bears too great a mind.' Shakespeare has him close the door on one kind of self-killing while leaving open the one he will actually choose. The position is unstable; 5.5 will resolve it.

trials.prompt: What does Octavius's 'I do not cross you; but I will do so' signal?
options: The first moment Octavius overrules Antony — a one-line foreshadowing of the Augustan settlement in which the 'peevish schoolboy' of 19 will outlast Antony and become first Roman emperor

trials.prompt: Cassius's speech about the eagles and the ravens reveals:
options: His philosophical defection — the committed Epicurean who mocked omens (1.2, 1.3) is now reading bird-flight as portent; the play shows philosophy failing to hold against the circumstances of its test

trials.prompt: 'Lovers in peace, lead on our days to age' uses 'lovers' in which sense?
options: Devoted friends — the Elizabethan sense, the same usage Brutus makes in 3.2's 'Romans, countrymen, and lovers' and Artemidorus uses in 2.3

trials.prompt: Brutus's answer to 'what will you do if we lose?' is contradictory — he argues against suicide but refuses to be led in triumph. This instability foreshadows:
options: His suicide in 5.5 — the Stoic case against self-killing that he cited against Cato is about to be revised under the actual pressure of defeat
